integration.body: Sprint = one compressed loop (5 days). Lean = all the loops (ongoing). Use Sprint for a specific idea. Use Lean for continuous cadence.

influenceCialdini.when: You need to persuade stakeholders, win buy-in, or design a marketing strategy — but you're relying on intuition instead of proven psychological principles.
influenceCialdini.why: Cialdini's 7 principles (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity, unity) are the evidence-based levers of persuasion. Each has 40+ years of experimental research behind it. A SaaS startup used this framework to redesign their pricing page and increased conversion 34% by adding social proof and scarcity cues.
influenceCialdini.how: Use the Decision Tree to pick the right principle for your situation. Each principle section has: Mechanism (how it works), 3 Key Experiments (replicated findings), Universal Patterns (when it works), and Defenses (when it fails or backfires). The Defense Audit section lets you stress-test your persuasion strategy against each principle's failure modes.
influenceCialdini.example: A nonprofit fundraising campaign applied the reciprocity principle: instead of asking for donations first, they sent a personalized hand-written thank-you card (unexpected gift). Donations increased 52% vs the control group. Key: the gift was unexpected, personalized, and not contingent on a donation — making it genuine reciprocity.
influenceCialdini.compare: vs Made to Stick: make ideas STICKY vs make people SAY YES · vs Storybrand: structure the STORY vs structure the ASK

skill.title: Your Persuasion Audit
skill.body: For YOUR current campaign/page/email, check each principle: RECIPROCITY — Are you giving something genuine first? ___ AUTHORITY — Are your credentials real and visible? ___ SOCIAL PROOF — Is the proof authentic and current? ___ SCARCITY — Is the scarcity genuine (not manufactured)? ___ COMMITMENT — Are you getting a small yes first? ___ LIKING — Are you being authentic? ___ UNITY — Are you creating real belonging? ___ Which principle are YOU strongest on? Which are you missing?

skill.title: Your Principle Selection
skill.body: For YOUR specific situation, pick 2-3 principles: (1) Are you giving something first? → Reciprocity. (2) Need public buy-in? → Social Proof. (3) Time-sensitive ask? → Scarcity. (4) Need trust? → Authority. (5) Partially committed audience? → Commitment. (6) Shared group identity? → Unity. (7) Need them to like you? → Liking. YOUR top 2-3 principles: ___. Why these: ___.

skill.title: Your Defense Check
skill.body: Before launching, stress-test: Is YOUR social proof real or cherry-picked? ___ Is YOUR scarcity genuine or manufactured? ___ Would an expert disagree with YOUR authority claims? ___ Are you exploiting sunk cost with commitment? ___ If any answer is risky, fix it BEFORE launching. Backlash destroys more value than the tactic creates.

skill.title: Your Touchpoint Stack
skill.body: For YOUR next customer touchpoint (email, page, ad), layer max 3 principles: Principle 1: ___ (how you'll apply it: ___). Principle 2: ___ (how: ___). Principle 3: ___ (how: ___). Rule: more than 3 = manipulation. Keep it ethical.

examples.title: Social Proof + Scarcity Pattern
examples.body: Pattern: '12,000+ teams trust us' (social proof) + 'Only 50 beta spots' (scarcity) → 34% conversion lift. Your turn: what's YOUR social proof? What's YOUR genuine scarcity?

examples.title: Reciprocity → Commitment Pattern
examples.body: Pattern: Give free value (report) → prove others used it (social proof) → ask for small commitment. Your turn: what can YOU give first? What small ask follows?

examples.title: Liking → Authority → Social Proof Pattern
examples.body: Pattern: Personal story (liking) → credibility (authority) → '3 companies here use this' (social proof). Your turn: what's YOUR opening story? Your credibility? Your proof?

casestudy.title: Booking.com — 7 principles simultaneously
casestudy.body: Social proof: '37 people looking.' Scarcity: 'Only 2 rooms left.' Authority: '150M+ guests.' Converts 3-5x higher than single-principle competitors. Your question: which principles are YOU using together?

integration.title: Influence × Made to Stick
integration.body: Made to Stick = make ideas MEMORABLE. Influence = make people ACT. Design the message first, then layer persuasion architecture.

integration.title: Influence × Storybrand
integration.body: Storybrand = structure the STORY (customer is hero). Influence = structure the ASK (psychological levers). Write the story, then add principles.

$100mLeads.when: You need a systematic lead generation engine, not random marketing tactics.
$100mLeads.why: The Core Four framework (warm outreach, content, cold outreach, paid ads) plus Rule of 100 and More-Better-New gives you a repeatable playbook — Hormozi's system turned $0 into $100M+ across multiple businesses.
$100mLeads.how: The $100M Leads output becomes your acquisition playbook. The Core Four matrix tells you which channel to start with. The Rule of 100 gives you daily minimums. More-Better-New tells you how to scale. Lead Getters (referrals, affiliates) let others generate leads for you.
$100mLeads.example: Gym Launch (Hormozi's company): Started with warm outreach to gym owners. Mastered cold outreach (100 calls/day). Added paid ads once unit economics worked. Layered in Lead Getters (referral program + agency model). Result: $100M+ in revenue from lead generation systems, not random tactics.
$100mLeads.compare: vs Traction: Core Four + Rule of 100 + More-Better-New vs 19 channels · vs GTM Engine: acquisition-focused playbook vs full revenue engine

skill.title: Your Starting Channel
skill.body: Answer: Do you have money? ___ Do you have time? ___ Money + Time → Paid Ads. Money + No Time → Paid Ads. No Money + Time → Warm Outreach. No Money + No Time → Content. YOUR starting channel: ___. Why: ___. Rule: master ONE before adding a second.

skill.title: Your Rule of 100
skill.body: Pick YOUR channel. Commit to 100 actions/day for 30 days. YOUR channel: ___. 100 actions/day means: ___ (100 calls? 100 DMs? 100 posts? $100/day ads?). 30-day target: 3,000 touches. At 1% conversion = 30 leads. Enough to learn what works. YOUR daily commitment: ___.

skill.title: Your More-Better-New Audit
skill.body: CURRENT STATE: What's already working? ___. How can you do MORE of it (100→200→500/day)? ___. How can you do BETTER (better script, offer, landing page)? ___. When should you add NEW? ___ (only after maxing More + Better). Most people jump to NEW. Don't.

skill.title: Your LTGP:CAC Math
skill.body: LTGP = (Avg Revenue × Gross Margin %) × Avg Lifespan in Months. YOUR LTGP: $___. YOUR CAC: $___. Ratio: ___:1. Target: 3:1+. Below 3:1 → fix unit economics before scaling. Above 5:1 → you're underinvesting in growth.

skill.title: Your Lead Magnet
skill.body: Formula: [Number] + [Adjective] + [Promise] + [Format]. YOUR lead magnet: 'The ___ [adjective] ___ for ___ [promise]'. Examples: 'The 7-Step Framework for Doubling Close Rate'. Bad: 'Our Whitepaper'. YOUR turn: write yours.

skill.title: Your $0→$100M Phase
skill.body: Which phase are YOU in? Phase 1 ($0-$100K): Warm outreach only. Phase 2 ($100K-$1M): Add cold outreach (100/day). Phase 3 ($1M-$10M): Add content. Phase 4 ($10M-$100M): Add paid ads + Lead Getters. YOUR phase: ___. Next channel to add: ___.

examples.title: Warm Outreach Pattern
examples.body: ACA: Acknowledge → Compliment → Ask. 'Hey [Name], saw your post about [topic] — really resonated. Your approach to [thing] is what most folks miss. Quick question: are you using anything for [problem]?' Expected reply: 15-25%. Your turn: write YOUR warm outreach message.

examples.title: Cold Email Pattern
examples.body: PAS: Problem → Agitate → Solution. 'Most [industry] teams spend 10+ hours/week on [task]. That's [X hours] of busywork. We built [Product] to automate this — 80% time savings in week 1. Want to see how?' Expected: 3-5% reply rate. Your turn: write YOUR cold email.

examples.title: Content Cadence Pattern
examples.body: Give, Give, Give, Ask. Post 1: useful framework (no ask). Post 2: case study (no ask). Post 3: tool/resource (no ask). Post 4: personal lesson THEN ask for lead magnet. Your turn: plan YOUR next 4 posts.

casestudy.title: Hormozi — $0 to $100M in 4 years
casestudy.body: Rule of 100: 100 content pieces/month. No paid ads for 2 years. GIVE→GIVE→GIVE→ASK. Your question: are YOU doing 100 actions/day in YOUR channel?

integration.title: $100M Leads × GTM Engine
integration.body: GTM Engine = full revenue system. $100M Leads = acquisition piece only. Use GTM Engine to find WHERE you're broken, $100M Leads to fix lead gen.

integration.title: $100M Leads × Traction
integration.body: Traction = 19 channels, test them. $100M Leads = 4 channels (Core Four), scale them. Use Traction for discovery, $100M Leads for scaling the winner.

rag.when: You need a baseline retrieval-augmented generation system before choosing specialized variants.
rag.why: Vanilla RAG (retrieve chunks, inject into prompt, generate) is the foundation — understand it before adding CRAG, GraphRAG, Hybrid, or Multimodal layers.

blueOcean.skill: Strategic framework for creating uncontested market space that makes the competition irrelevant, based on the simultaneous pursuit of differentiation and low cost. Core Principle: Don’t compete in bloody red oceans. Create blue oceans of uncontested market space. Most companies fight for share in existing industries; winners create new market space where competition is irrelevant by delivering a leap in value for both buyers and themselves.
blueOcean.framework: Four-gate Strategic Sequence: Buyer Utility → Strategic Price → Target Cost → Adoption. Six Paths: Alternative Industries, Strategic Groups, Chain of Buyers, Complementary Products, Functional-Emotional Appeal, Time/Trends. ERRC Grid: Eliminate, Reduce, Raise, Create. Three Tiers of Non-Customers: Soon-to-be, Refusing, Unexplored. Strategy Canvas with divergent value curve as diagnostic.
blueOcean.examples: Cirque du Soleil: eliminated animals/stars, created themed art circus — priced above circus, costs below theater. Yellow Tail: eliminated terroir/complexity — #1 imported wine. Nintendo Wii: eliminated graphics race, created motion control — 101M units. Southwest: competed against car travel, not airlines — 45+ years profitable. Netflix: eliminated late fees/stores, created streaming — 230M+ subscribers. Uber: eliminated hailing/cash, created GPS tracking — gig economy pioneer.
blueOcean.casestudy: Cirque du Soleil: blended circus arts with theater, targeting adults/corporate clients. Revenue exceeded Ringling Bros within 20 years. Southwest Airlines: competed against Greyhound and the family car. Largest domestic carrier by passengers. Key insight: your real competitor may not be who you think.
blueOcean.integration: Strategy Canvas × ERRC Grid × Six Paths × Non-Customers × Strategic Sequence × Value Innovation × Buyer Utility Map
blueOcean.when: Use when you want to CREATE new market space instead of competing. Best for: innovation strategy, breaking out of commodity traps, launching new categories.

mckinseyInfluence.when: You need to change stakeholder behavior, not just alignment.
mckinseyInfluence.why: McKinsey's Influence model (Understanding → Dedication → Reinforcement → Confidence) targets the four psychological barriers to behavior change — what people think, what they feel, what they experience, and what they believe they can do.

skill.title: Internal Inventory
skill.body: List your top 3-5 Strengths with evidence (data, metrics, testimonials). Then list top 3-5 Weaknesses — be honest about what competitors do better. Each item needs a source.

skill.title: External Scan
skill.body: List 3-5 Opportunities from market trends, tech shifts, or competitor gaps. List 3-5 Threats from regulation, macro forces, or new entrants. Use external data sources.

skill.title: TOWS Cross-Analysis
skill.body: For each Strength, ask: which Opportunities can it capture (SO)? For each Weakness, ask: which Opportunities does it block (WO)? Map all four quadrants.

skill.title: Prioritize Actions
skill.body: Score each item Impact (1-5) x Urgency (1-5). Top 3 items become quarterly action plan. Assign owner + deadline to each.

examples.title: Netflix 2011
examples.body: SWOT revealed DVD-by-mail was the dying weakness while streaming was the growing opportunity — pivot was undeniable.

examples.title: Retailer Channel Shift
examples.body: A retailer thought the rival was the real threat. SWOT revealed the actual threat was channel shift to e-commerce, not the rival.

casestudy.title: Qwikster Debacle
casestudy.body: Netflix SWOT was right but execution was wrong — splitting DVD/streaming into separate brands caused a 77% subscriber loss before reversal. SWOT is only as good as the action plan.

integration.title: SWOT x PESTEL
integration.body: PESTEL fills the Opportunities and Threats quadrants with macro data.

integration.title: SWOT x Porter Five Forces
integration.body: Porter fills the Threats quadrant with industry structure analysis.

integration.title: SWOT x TOWS Matrix
integration.body: TOWS is the systematic cross-analysis extension of SWOT.

swot.when: Use when you need a quick internal+external scan. SWOT is a starting point, not a strategy. Best for: initial situation assessment, team alignment on where you stand.

skill.title: External Scan
skill.body: Score each PESTEL factor 1-5 for impact on your business. Mark trend direction (up/stable/down). For each factor scored 4-5, write a concrete response action.

skill.title: Strategic Response
skill.body: For every high-impact factor: define a response action, assign an owner, set a deadline. Low-impact factors get passive monitoring only.

examples.title: GDPR Compliance
examples.body: A SaaS firm scored Legal (GDPR) at 5 — invested 6 months early, gained competitive advantage over slower rivals.

examples.title: Regulation Spotting
examples.body: A firm spotted a regulation risk through PESTEL in time to pivot before it became a crisis.

casestudy.title: Macro Blindspot
casestudy.body: A company focused only on competitors (internal) and missed a regulatory shift that restructured their entire market. PESTEL would have caught it 18 months earlier.

integration.title: PESTEL x SWOT
integration.body: PESTEL provides the external data for the O and T quadrants of SWOT.

integration.title: PESTEL x Scenario Planning
integration.body: PESTEL factors become the variables in scenario planning models.

pestle.when: Use when external forces (political, economic, social, tech, legal, environmental) are shaping your challenge. Best for: market entry, regulatory changes, macro trend analysis.

skill.title: Force Mapping
skill.body: Rate each force 1-5. Add key evidence for each score. The strongest force dictates your strategy. Total score: /25. Above 15 = unattractive industry.

skill.title: Strategic Response
skill.body: For the strongest force: fight it (differentiation), bypass it (niche), or exploit gaps (cost advantage). Define your response and owner.

examples.title: Cloud Computing
examples.body: High supplier power (AWS/Azure oligopoly) + low buyer power (switching costs) = suppliers set the terms. Strategy: multi-cloud.

examples.title: Restaurant Industry
examples.body: Low barriers to entry + high rivalry + low switching costs = terrible economics. Strategy: differentiation through brand/experience.

casestudy.title: Power Squeeze
casestudy.body: A firm in a power-squeezed segment used Five Forces to identify the exit was the only viable strategy — they repositioned before margins collapsed.

integration.title: Porter x SWOT
integration.body: Five Forces analysis feeds the Threats quadrant of SWOT with industry structure data.

integration.title: Porter x Competitive Landscape
integration.body: Competitive Landscape maps specific rivals; Five Forces maps the structural forces those rivals operate within.

porter.when: Use when you need to understand industry attractiveness and competitive dynamics. Best for: deciding which markets to enter, assessing profit potential, competitive positioning.

skill.title: Set Objective
skill.body: Write ONE qualitative, ambitious, time-bound objective. No metrics in the objective — those go in Key Results. Should inspire and align the team.

skill.title: Define Key Results
skill.body: Write 2-4 measurable Key Results per Objective. Each needs: baseline, target, and current tracking. Score 0.0-1.0 at quarter end. Target average: 0.6-0.7.

skill.title: Weekly Check-in
skill.body: Every week: review KR progress, identify blockers, adjust tactics. Monthly: review confidence levels. Quarterly: score all OKRs and set next quarter.

examples.title: Google 10x OKRs
examples.body: Google uses OKRs at all levels. Individual contributors set OKRs that roll up to team, which roll up to company. Score of 0.7 is considered success.

casestudy.title: Vanity OKRs
casestudy.body: A startup set OKRs that were all already achievable (score 1.0 consistently) — they were sandbagging. OKR system only works when goals are genuinely ambitious.

integration.title: OKR x North Star Metric
integration.body: North Star Metric becomes the primary Key Result for the company-level Objective.

integration.title: OKR x Balanced Scorecard
integration.body: Balanced Scorecard perspectives map to different Objective categories (Financial, Customer, Process, Learning).

okrFw.when: Use when you need to align team around measurable outcomes, not tasks. Best for: quarterly planning, team alignment, performance tracking.

skill.title: Score Components
skill.body: For each feature: Reach (people/quarter), Impact (0.25-3), Confidence (50/80/100%), Effort (person-months). RICE = (R x I x C) / E. Sort descending.

skill.title: Commit to Top Items
skill.body: Top 20% of features deliver 80% of value. For each top-tier feature: define MVP scope, assign owner, set deadline. Kill or defer everything below median.

examples.title: Intercom Roadmap Cut
examples.body: Intercom (RICE creators) cut 60+ features to 12. Feature A (score 600) beat B (score 288) despite B having higher reach — because B needed 5x the effort.

casestudy.title: Pet Project Override
casestudy.body: A CEO overrode RICE scores to ship a pet project. It scored 88 vs the top feature at 600. The pet project generated zero revenue; the RICE top pick drove 40% of growth.

integration.title: RICE x Kano Model
integration.body: Kano identifies feature types (basic/performance/delighter); RICE prioritizes within each type.

integration.title: RICE x Lean Canvas
integration.body: Lean Canvas Key Metrics become the Impact targets in RICE scoring.

rice.when: Use when you need to objectively prioritize competing initiatives. Best for: roadmap decisions, resource allocation, sprint planning. More rigorous than gut-feel.

skill.title: Map the Person
skill.body: Identify who you are mapping (name/role). Fill Say (quotes), Think (beliefs), Feel (emotions), Do (observed actions). Look for Say-Do gaps — that is where insights live.

skill.title: Pains & Gains
skill.body: List frustrations/risks (Pains) and desired outcomes (Gains). Each Pain maps to your Pain Relievers. Each Gain maps to your Gain Creators. Update after every 3 interviews.

examples.title: Airbnb Photography
examples.body: Airbnb hosts feared my place will look bad. The empathy map led to free professional photography that doubled bookings.

casestudy.title: Say-Do Gap
casestudy.body: A product team surveyed users who said they wanted Feature X. The empathy map revealed they never actually used similar features. Building X was a waste — observe behavior, not stated preferences.

integration.title: Empathy Map x Persona
integration.body: Empathy Map captures what a persona SAYS/THINKS/FEELS/DOES; Persona defines who they ARE.

integration.title: Empathy Map x Value Proposition Canvas
integration.body: Pains map to Pain Relievers; Gains map to Gain Creators in the VPC.

empathy.when: Use when you need to deeply understand what customers think, feel, say, and do. Best for: early discovery, user research, building personas.

skill.title: Element Inventory
skill.body: For each of the 7 S elements: describe current state, desired state, gap (1-5), and action to close. Shared Values is the center — all others must align to it.

skill.title: Alignment Scoring
skill.body: Score each element 1-5 for alignment with Shared Values. Sum /30. Below 10 = aligned. Above 18 = significant misalignment. Fix the lowest-scoring element first.

examples.title: Bank Digital Transformation
examples.body: Strategy said digital, but incentives still rewarded branch sales. 7S revealed the misalignment — fixing incentives first unlocked the transformation.

casestudy.title: Strategy-Structure Mismatch
casestudy.body: A company pursued a matrix strategy but kept a functional structure. The 7S model would have flagged this before the 18-month reorg that followed.

integration.title: 7S x SWOT
integration.body: 7S fills the Weaknesses quadrant of SWOT with specific internal misalignments.

integration.title: 7S x Change Management
integration.body: 7S diagnostic drives the change agenda — misaligned elements become the change targets.

skill.title: Phase Progression
skill.body: Do NOT skip steps. Score each 0-100%. Any step below 50% = go back and fix before proceeding. Assign owner and deadline to every step.

skill.title: Quick Win Planning
skill.body: Steps 1-5 create the climate. Step 6 (Quick Wins) is the make-or-break moment. Engineer 2-3 visible wins in the first 60 days of the change effort.

examples.title: Change Momentum
examples.body: A transformation that skipped Quick Wins lost momentum by month 4. Restarting with a 30-day visible win campaign reignited the effort.

casestudy.title: Skip-the-Steps Failure
casestudy.body: A CEO announced a vision (Step 3) without building urgency (Step 1) or coalition (Step 2). The change was ignored within weeks.

integration.title: Kotter x ADKAR
integration.body: Kotter operates at the organizational level; ADKAR operates at the individual level. Use both for complete change coverage.

integration.title: Kotter x 7S
integration.body: Kotter steps address the alignment gaps that 7S reveals — especially Structure, Systems, and Style.

skill.title: Base-Up Diagnosis
skill.body: Score each dysfunction 1-5. Fix the lowest-scoring dysfunction at the base FIRST. You CANNOT fix upper layers without fixing the ones below. Trust is always the starting point.

skill.title: Team Assessment
skill.body: Use anonymous surveys to get honest scores. Common trap: teams score trust high but accountability low — often the trust is表面的, not genuine vulnerability.

examples.title: Trust Unlock
examples.body: A leadership team scored high on trust but low on accountability. Root cause: trust was表面的. Building real trust (vulnerability exercises) unlocked healthy accountability.

casestudy.title: Artificial Harmony
casestudy.body: A team avoided all conflict, scored high on harmony. The result: decisions were never truly committed to, deadlines slipped, and accountability was impossible. Healthy conflict is a feature, not a bug.

integration.title: Lencioni x OKR
integration.body: Lencioni diagnoses why teams fail to hit OKRs — usually a dysfunction at the trust or commitment level.

integration.title: Lencioni x 7S
integration.body: 7S reveals organizational misalignment; Lencioni reveals team behavioral dysfunction. Both are needed.

skill.title: Customer Profile
skill.body: Map Customer Jobs (what they try to do), Pains (what frustrates them), Gains (what they want). Rank each by importance/severity. Use interview data, not assumptions.

skill.title: Value Map Fit
skill.body: Map Products & Services, Pain Relievers, Gain Creators. Score fit 1-5 for each pair. Below 3 = redesign needed. The 1:1 mapping between relievers and pains creates product-market fit.

examples.title: Feature-Pain Mismatch
examples.body: A SaaS team discovered their top feature addressed a gain rated 2/5 importance. Pivoting to a pain rated 5/5 tripled conversion.

casestudy.title: Gain Creator Without Pain
casestudy.body: A team built a sophisticated analytics dashboard (gain creator) but customers had no analytics pain. The dashboard was unused. Pain-first design wins.

integration.title: VPC x Lean Canvas
integration.body: VPC provides the detailed customer-value fit; Lean Canvas provides the full business model context.

integration.title: VPC x Empathy Map
integration.body: Empathy Map outputs (pains, gains, jobs) map directly into the Customer Profile side of the VPC.

valuePropCanvas.when: Use when you need to match your product to customer needs precisely. Best for: product-market fit validation, messaging, feature alignment.

skill.title: Loop Mapping
skill.body: Map your loop: User Action → Output → Distribution → Growth → Loop Back. Identify the trigger, the output, the distribution channel, and the loop-back mechanism.

skill.title: Bottleneck Analysis
skill.body: Measure conversion at each stage. The stage with the lowest conversion rate is your bottleneck. Invest there first. Target: loop-back rate > 20% for healthy compounding.

examples.title: Dropbox Referral Loop
examples.body: User invites friend → friend signs up → both get storage → new user invites more. K-factor > 1 = viral growth. The storage incentive was the loop-back mechanism.

casestudy.title: Paid Loop Death Spiral
casestudy.body: A company scaled paid acquisition (paid loop) without fixing retention. LTV:CAC went negative. The loop was leaky — growth was actually value-destroying.

integration.title: Growth Loops x AARRR
integration.body: AARRR measures funnel stages; Growth Loops shows how output feeds back into input for compounding.

integration.title: Growth Loops x North Star
integration.body: North Star Metric should be the output metric of your primary growth loop.

skill.title: Feature Classification
skill.body: Use the Kano questionnaire (functional + dysfunctional pairs) to classify features. Must-Be = table stakes. Performance = compete on. Delighter = differentiate with.

skill.title: Investment Prioritization
skill.body: 1) All Must-Be features must work. 2) Performance features: invest where you lead. 3) Delighters: pick 1-2 that align with differentiation. A delighter today becomes must-be tomorrow.

examples.title: Hidden Must-Be
examples.body: A team classified a feature as a Delighter, but customer surveys revealed it was actually a Must-Be — users expected it and were frustrated it was missing. Kano saved them from the wrong investment.

casestudy.title: Delighter Over-Investment
casestudy.body: A team spent 60% of engineering on delighters while must-be features were buggy. Customer satisfaction dropped. Fix the table stakes first.

integration.title: Kano x RICE
integration.body: Kano classifies feature types; RICE prioritizes within each type by reach x impact x confidence / effort.

integration.title: Kano x Value Proposition Canvas
integration.body: Must-Be features map to Pain Relievers; Delighters map to Gain Creators in the VPC.

kano.when: Use when you need to understand which features delight vs. disappoint vs. are expected. Best for: product design, feature prioritization, understanding customer satisfaction dynamics.

skill: Given: nums=[2,7,11,15], target=9 | Find: indices of pair summing to target | Constraints: n<=10^4, O(n) required
Structure: ARRAY (pair with target) → Decision Matrix → Two Pointer algorithm, L/R pointers
Intermediate DS: Two integer variables L=0, R=n-1; no heap, no map needed for sorted input
Build: sort if unsorted → initialize L=0, R=n-1 → while L<R: if sum<target L++ else R--
Verify: check edge cases (duplicates, negative numbers, single pair)
examples: Two Sum (pair sum): HashMap for O(n) unsorted, Two Pointer for O(n) sorted
Sliding Window Max: deque maintains indices of decreasing elements — each element enters/exits deque once → O(n)

casestudy.title: Coin Change Greedy Trap
casestudy.body: Greedy fails for coins=[1,3,4] amount=6: greedy gives 4+1+1=3 coins, but DP gives 3+3=2 coins. Always test greedy before assuming it works — use DP when unsure.

integration: dsPatternFinder × Identify pattern first, then use Decision Matrix for algorithm selection
dsAlgoRouter × Route from matrix result to detailed implementation template
dsAlgoDetails × Get code template and edge cases for the chosen algorithm

skill: Score your message on SUCCESs (1-5 per principle): Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories
Find the broken principle: "Too complicated" = Simple, "They tune out" = Unexpected, "They forget" = Concrete
Fix lowest 1-2 principles first: Commander's Intent for Simple, counterintuitive hook for Unexpected, sensory details for Concrete
Apply case studies: Southwest (Simple), Nordstrom tires (Unexpected), Boeing 727 (Concrete), Safexpress HP (Credible)
Diagnose: "They believe but dont care" = Emotional, "They care but dont act" = Stories (Springboard Story)
examples: Southwest: "THE low-cost airline" = Commander's Intent, 47 years profit, 70K employees aligned by one sentence
Nordstrom: accepted tire return they dont sell tires = Unexpected schema violation, legendary without marketing budget
Boeing 727: "Seat 131, Miami to NYC, Runway 4-22 La Guardia" = Concrete target aligned thousands of design decisions
Safexpress: delivered Harry Potter to every India bookstore on release day = Sinatra Test won Bollywood deal
"Dont Mess with Texas": identity appeal (Texan pride) not environmental logic = 72% litter reduction in 2 years
World Bank: Zambia health worker story launched knowledge management transformation where data failed

casestudy.title: Rokia Study (Carnegie Mellon)
casestudy.body: Statistics alone (millions suffering): $1.14 avg donation. One named girl (Rokia, 7, Mali): $2.38. Both combined: $1.43. Analytical priming before appeal: -46% giving. The analytical frame of mind is the enemy of empathy. Lead with ONE person, not statistics.

casestudy.title: Subway "Sandwich Artists" (Bad Analogy)
casestudy.body: Disney "cast members" changes behavior across thousands of novel situations. Subway "sandwich artists" doesnt change how anyone makes a sandwich. A generative analogy must produce NEW correct inferences on its own. Test: Does the analogy help someone make correct decisions in situations you never described?

integration: Influence (Cialdini 7) x Apply SUCCESs principles to make your influence messages memorable and sticky
Storybrand x Structure the STORY first (SUCCESs Stories), then apply the full SUCCESs framework
Blue Ocean Strategy x Use Simple + Concrete to define your new value curve in one sentence
madeToStick.when: Use when you need to make your message memorable using the SUCCESs principles. Best for: presentations, pitches, internal communications, marketing.

porterGeneric.when: Use when choosing HOW to compete: cost leadership, differentiation, or focus. Best for: making a fundamental strategic choice about your competitive approach.

jtbd.when: Use when you need to understand what job customers are hiring your product to do. Best for: product strategy, feature definition, finding unmet needs.

momTest.when: Use when you need to validate business ideas through customer conversations WITHOUT leading them. Best for: early-stage validation, avoiding false positives.

leanStartup.when: Use when building something new and need to learn fast through Build-Measure-Learn loops. Best for: MVP testing, product-market fit, iterative development.

fourSteps.when: Use when taking a product from idea to scale and need to know which phase you are in. Best for: startup sequencing, avoiding premature scaling.

balancedScorecard.when: Use when you need a balanced view across financial, customer, process, and learning metrics. Best for: executive reporting, strategy alignment, holistic performance.

scamper.when: Use when you need structured creativity to improve existing products or processes. Best for: product improvement, process optimization, creative brainstorming with discipline.

starburst.when: Use when you need to explore a topic from every angle through questioning. Best for: problem exploration, strategy sessions, challenging assumptions.

influenceCialdini.when: Use when you need to understand and apply ethical persuasion. Best for: marketing, sales, negotiations, leadership communication.

frameworkCompare.5: vs Fishbone: trace ONE root-cause chain, not a category map

swot.compare: vs PESTEL: SWOT is internal+external quick scan; PESTEL is deep external analysis. vs Porter: SWOT is self-assessment; Porter is industry attractiveness. vs Blue Ocean: SWOT finds your position; Blue Ocean creates new space.

porter.compare: vs SWOT: Porter analyzes industry structure; SWOT analyzes your position. vs Blue Ocean: Porter helps you compete in existing markets; Blue Ocean helps you escape them.

pestle.compare: vs SWOT: PESTEL is external-only and deeper; SWOT includes internal. Use PESTEL when external forces dominate your challenge.

frameworkCompare.bcg: vs Porter: allocate a PORTFOLIO by growth/share, not assess industry attractiveness
frameworkCompare.canvas: vs BCG: design the whole value model, not just prioritize business units
frameworkCompare.aiValue: vs Canvas: tie AI bets to P&L levers, not just describe the model
frameworkCompare.opModel: vs Canvas: turn strategy into how work actually runs (process, tech, locations)
frameworkCompare.businessPlan: vs Canvas: a one-page plan to win funders, not the full model

blueOcean.compare: vs Porter: Porter helps you win in existing markets; Blue Ocean creates new ones. vs SWOT: Blue Ocean is forward-looking and creative; SWOT is diagnostic.

frameworkCompare.minto: vs SCQA: lead with the ANSWER · vs Issue Tree: the communication, not the analysis
frameworkCompare.scqa: vs Minto: open with Situation-Complication-Question before the answer
frameworkCompare.mece: vs Logic Tree: the cleanliness RULE that makes any breakdown valid
frameworkCompare.mckIssueTree: vs Logic Tree: same discipline, McKinsey-named · vs Hyp Tree: structure first
frameworkCompare.mckHypTree: vs Issue Tree: emphasizes TESTABLE hypotheses over pure structure
frameworkCompare.mckProblemStmt: vs Minto: nail the problem before building the pyramid
frameworkCompare.mckPSP: vs Minto: the one-page DELIVERABLE of the pyramid (governing thought → support)
frameworkCompare.mckStakeholder: vs Influence maps: name exactly who DECIDES and who can block
frameworkCompare.mckinseyInfluence: vs 7S: behavior-change levers, not structural alignment · vs ADKAR: four SIMULTANEOUS levers vs a sequential individual journey
frameworkCompare.mckWorkplan: vs Roadmap: week-by-week delivery, not what/when at a glance
frameworkCompare.mckDistinct: vs Positioning: force a memorable "only we" claim customers recall
frameworkCompare.mckMethod: vs others: the engagement OPERATING SYSTEM, not a single tool
frameworkCompare.first: vs SCAMPER: re-derive from first principles, don't remix what exists

scamper.compare: vs Starbursting: SCAMPER gives you specific actions (Substitute, Combine, etc.); Starbursting gives you question categories (Who, What, Where, etc.). SCAMPER for improving; Starbursting for exploring.

frameworkCompare.logic: vs MECE: a hierarchical ARGUMENT · MECE is the rule that keeps it clean
frameworkCompare.hats: vs Diamond: six FIXED lenses (facts/feelings/creative…) vs free diverge-converge
frameworkCompare.diamond: vs Hats: diverge-then-converge with no fixed roles
frameworkCompare.fishbone: vs 5 Whys: map many cause CATEGORIES, not one root-cause chain
frameworkCompare.issue: vs Logic Tree: a decision-tree framing of the same structured discipline

starburst.compare: vs SCAMPER: Starbursting is question-driven (explore from all angles); SCAMPER is action-driven (specific transformation moves). Starbursting for opening up; SCAMPER for changing things.

jtbd.compare: vs Empathy: JTBD focuses on the job, not the person. vs Value Prop Canvas: JTBD defines what job to solve; VPC maps how your product solves it.

frameworkCompare.value: vs Canvas: the customer-value HALF of the business model

kano.compare: vs RICE: Kano tells you WHAT type each feature is (must-be/performance/delighter); RICE tells you the effort-adjusted priority. Use both together.

rice.compare: vs Kano: RICE scores by impact/effort; Kano classifies by satisfaction type. Use RICE to rank items, Kano to understand what each item does to satisfaction.

frameworkCompare.northstar: vs OKR: ONE leading metric vs objectives + key results
frameworkCompare.okr: vs North Star: set Objectives + Key Results, not a single metric
frameworkCompare.pmf: vs Traction: prove DEMAND exists before hunting the channel
frameworkCompare.traction: vs Pirate: the 19 channels to find ONE repeatable engine
frameworkCompare.pirate: vs Traction: the AARRR funnel lens on growth
frameworkCompare.leancanvas: vs Canvas: startup risk-forward version (problems before polish)
frameworkCompare.dvf: vs Lean Canvas: the desirability×feasibility×viability gate
frameworkCompare.roadmap: vs Workplan: what/when/why at a glance, not week-by-week

empathy.compare: vs JTBD: Empathy Map captures feelings/thoughts; JTBD captures the functional job. vs Value Prop Canvas: Empathy is discovery; Value Prop Canvas is alignment.

frameworkCompare.persona: vs Empathy Map: a concrete archetype vs a feeling map
frameworkCompare.customer: vs JTBD: the end-to-end journey vs the job hired
frameworkCompare.micro: vs Modular: independent SERVICES vs internal modules
frameworkCompare.rest: vs Event/Graph: uniform resource style vs flows/relationships
frameworkCompare.event: vs REST: event-driven flows vs request/response resources
frameworkCompare.sysdesign: vs Micro: the scalability/consistency TRADE-OFF conversation
frameworkCompare.togaf: vs others: a repeatable ENTERPRISE architecture method
frameworkCompare.rag: baseline retrieval-augmented generation before the specialized variants below
frameworkCompare.crag: vs vanilla RAG: self-CHECK retrieval quality before answering
frameworkCompare.graphrag: vs RAG: reason over RELATIONSHIPS, not just text chunks
frameworkCompare.hybridrag: vs RAG: keyword + semantic for higher recall AND precision
frameworkCompare.multimodalrag: vs RAG: answer over images/tables/audio, not just text
frameworkCompare.agenticrag: vs RAG: plan/act/reflect AGENTS over retrieved context
frameworkCompare.multiagent: vs Agentic RAG: several SPECIALIZED agents hand off vs one
frameworkCompare.tooluse: vs Agents: just the structured tool INTERFACE, not autonomy
frameworkCompare.reflection: vs Agents: a self-critique LOOP, not multi-agent handoff
frameworkCompare.scopeai: vs others: pin the AI project's scope & success criteria first
frameworkCompare.ai: vs AI Value: the pattern MAP vs the value map
frameworkCompare.pixar: vs Storybrand: character ARC vs customer-as-hero
frameworkCompare.storybrand: vs Pixar: you are the GUIDE, customer is the hero
frameworkCompare.hero: vs Pitch: the STORY vs the ask
frameworkCompare.pitch: vs Hero: the ASK vs the story
frameworkCompare.influenceCialdini: vs Made to Stick: make ideas STICKY vs make people SAY YES · vs Storybrand: structure the STORY vs structure the ASK

fourSteps.compare: vs Lean Startup: Four Steps gives you the roadmap of phases; Lean Startup gives you the engine of each phase. Use Four Steps to know WHERE you are; Lean Startup to know WHAT to do.

frameworkCompare.thesis: vs Minto: the single CLAIM vs the full pyramid
frameworkCompare.galbraithStar: vs Weisbord: the five design LEVERS (strategy/structure/process/reward/people)
frameworkCompare.weisbord6box: vs Nadler-Tushman: six-box diagnostic vs congruence/fit view
frameworkCompare.burkeLitwin: vs Nadler-Tushman: separates TRANSFORMATION vs steady-state drivers
frameworkCompare.nadlerTushman: vs Burke-Litwin: congruence/fit of parts vs the causal drivers
frameworkCompare.mintzbergConfig: vs Lawrence-Lorsch: whole-config shapes vs differentiation/integration
frameworkCompare.lawrenceLorsch: vs Mintzberg: when to couple units tightly vs pick a config
frameworkCompare.gooldCampbell9: vs Goold Parenting: the nine TESTS vs the "where HQ adds value" question
frameworkCompare.gooldParenting: vs Goold Control: what the CENTER should add vs how tightly to control
frameworkCompare.ambidextrous: vs Functional: run exploit + explore as SEPARATE units
frameworkCompare.socioTechnical: vs Holacracy: optimize social+technical jointly vs replace managers
frameworkCompare.holacracy: vs Socio-Technical: distributed ROLES vs joint optimization
frameworkCompare.teamBasedOrg: vs Functional: cross-functional TEAMS vs deep specialties
frameworkCompare.bcgMatrix: vs BCG (Strategy): corporate PORTFOLIO of units vs single-business matrix
frameworkCompare.mckinseyGtm: vs GTM Engine: McKinsey's 6-pillar + 7-step structured process vs the gear metaphor
frameworkCompare.pricingStrategy: vs GTM Engine: dedicated pricing DEEP-DIVE vs one gear in the engine
frameworkCompare.competitiveLandscape: vs SWOT: competitive-focused with differentiation levers vs four-quadrant snapshot
frameworkCompare.personaDeepDive: vs Persona: multi-role BUYER archetypes with journey + messaging vs single user profile
frameworkCompare.salesEnablement: vs Messaging Hierarchy: sales-specific BATTLE CARDS + stage guidance vs brand-level message
frameworkCompare.feedbackLoops: vs GTM Metrics: structured REVIEW cadences + feedback channels vs raw KPI tracking
frameworkCompare.$100mLeads: vs Traction: Core Four + Rule of 100 + More-Better-New vs 19 channels · vs GTM Engine: acquisition-focused playbook vs full revenue engine
frameworkCompare.tradingComps: vs DCF: market-relative valuation vs intrinsic · vs LBO: private equity return profile vs public market multiples
frameworkCompare.ccc: vs DSO/DIO/DPO individually: NET cash cycle speed vs isolated metric

leanStartup.compare: vs Four Steps: Lean Startup is the loop (build-measure-learn); Four Steps is the sequence (search → scale). vs Mom Test: Lean Startup is the full process; Mom Test is the validation technique.

valuePropCanvas.compare: vs JTBD: VPC is the detailed matching tool; JTBD is the discovery tool. vs Empathy: VPC is prescriptive (what to build); Empathy is diagnostic (what they feel).

momTest.compare: vs JTBD: Mom Test validates if the problem is real; JTBD defines what the problem is. vs Lean Startup: Mom Test is the interview method; Lean Startup is the build-measure-learn loop.

okrFw.compare: vs Balanced Scorecard: OKRs are outcome-focused and quarterly; BSC is holistic and strategic. OKRs for team execution; BSC for executive strategy.

balancedScorecard.compare: vs OKR: BSC provides the 4-perspective lens (Financial/Customer/Process/L&G); OKRs provide the target-setting mechanism. BSC tells you what to measure; OKRs tell you what to achieve.
